volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: The Rope (Section 1.4.243b - Section 2.3.336c)

sections.label: Section 1.4.243b
sections.original: Áccipe .
sections.translation: Take it .

sections.label: Section 1.4.243c
sections.original: Díc , vivisne ? óbsecro .
sections.translation: Are you still alive ? Prithee , tell me .

sections.label: Section 1.4.244
sections.original: Tú facis mé quidem ut vívere núnc velim ,
sections.translation: You , indeed , make me now wish to live ,

sections.label: Section 1.4.245
sections.original: quóm mihi té licet tángere . ut víx mihi
sections.translation: since I ’ m empowered to touch you . How hardly can I persuade myself of this , that I am holding you . Prithee , do embrace me ( they embrace ) , my only hope ; how you are now easing me of all my woes .

sections.label: Section 1.4.248
sections.original: Óccupas praéloqui quaé mea orátiost .
sections.translation: You are beforehand with me in using expressions which belong to me . Now it befits us to be going hence .

sections.label: Section 1.4.249b
sections.original: Quo , amabo , íbimus ?
sections.translation: Prithee , whither shall we go ?

sections.label: Section 1.4.250
sections.original: Lítus hoc pérsequamúr .
sections.translation: Let ’ s keep along this sea-shore .

sections.label: Section 1.4.250b
sections.original: Sequor quó lubet .
sections.translation: Wherever you please , I ’ ll follow .

sections.label: Section 1.4.251
sections.original: sícine hic cum úvida véste grassábimur ?
sections.translation: Shall we go along thus with our wet clothing ?

sections.label: Section 1.4.252
sections.original: Hóc quod est , íd necessárium est pérpeti .
sections.translation: That which exists , the same must of necessity be borne . ( Looking up at the Temple .) But , pray , what ’ s this ?

sections.label: Section 1.4.253b
sections.original: Quid ?
sections.translation: What is it ?

sections.label: Section 1.4.253c
sections.original: Víden , amabo ,
sections.translation: Prithee , don ’ t you see this Temple ?

sections.label: Section 1.4.254b
sections.original: Vbi est ?
sections.translation: Where is it ?

sections.label: Section 1.4.254c
sections.original: Ad déxteram .
sections.translation: On the right hand .

sections.label: Section 1.4.255
sections.original: Videó decorum dís locum vidérier .
sections.translation: I seem to be looking at a place becoming the Divinities .

sections.label: Section 1.4.256
sections.original: Haud lónge abesse opórtet homines hínc , ita hic lepidúst locus .
sections.translation: There must be people not far hence ; it is so delightful a spot . Whoever the God is , I pray him to relieve us from these troubles , and to succour us females , wretched , helpless , and in distress .

sections.label: Section 1.5.259
sections.original: Qui súnt qui a patróna precés mea expetéssunt ?
sections.translation: Who are these , that in their prayers are soliciting aid from my Patroness ?

sections.label: Section 1.5.260
sections.original: nam vóx me precántum huc forás excitávit .
sections.translation: For the voice of suppliants has brought me hither out of doors . They pay suit to a kind and compliant Goddess and a Patroness that makes no difficulties , and one who is very benevolent .

sections.label: Section 1.5.263
sections.original: Iubémus te sálvere , máter .
sections.translation: Mother , we bid you hail .

sections.label: Section 1.5.263b
sections.original: Salvéte ,
sections.translation: Maidens , hail to you . But , prithee , whence

sections.label: Section 1.5.265
sections.original: vos íre cum uvida veste dicam , óbsecro , tam maéstiter vestítas ?
sections.translation: am I to say that you are hither come with your wet garments , thus wofully arrayed ?

sections.label: Section 1.5.266
sections.original: Ílico hinc ímus , haud lóngule ex hóc loco ;
sections.translation: Just now , we came from a place there ( pointing towards the shore ) , not a great way from this spot ; but it is a great way off from here , whence we have been brought hither .

sections.label: Section 1.5.268
sections.original: Némpe equo lígneo pér vias caérulas
sections.translation: Have you been borne , do you mean , by a ship , the wooden steed [*] , over the azure paths ?

sections.label: Section 1.5.269b
sections.original: Ádmodum .
sections.translation: Even so .

sections.label: Section 1.5.269c
sections.original: Ergo aéquius vós erat
sections.translation: Then it were more fitting that you should have come

sections.label: Section 1.5.270
sections.original: cándidatás venire hóstiatásque . ad hoc
sections.translation: arrayed in white and provided with victims ; it isn ’ t the practice for people to come to this Temple in that fashion .

sections.label: Section 1.5.272
sections.original: Quaéne eiectae é mari símus ambae , óbsecro ,
sections.translation: Prithee , whence would you have us , who have been both cast away at sea , to be bringing victims hither ? Now , in want of assistance , do we embrace your knees ,

sections.label: Section 1.5.275
sections.original: quae ín locis nésciis néscia spé sumus ,
sections.translation: we who are of hopes undefined in places unknown , that you may receive us under your roof and shelter us , and that you will pity the miseries of us both , who have neither any place of refuge nor hope at hand , nor have anything whatever of our own beyond that which you see .

sections.label: Section 1.5.280
sections.original: Manús mihi date , éxurgite á genibus ámbae .
sections.translation: Give me your hands , arise , both of you , from off your knees ; no one among women is more compassionate than I . ( They arise from the ground .) But , maidens , my circumstances are poor and limited ; with difficulty I support my own existence ; Venus I serve for my maintenance .

sections.label: Section 1.5.284
sections.original: Venerís fanum , obsecro , hóc est ?
sections.translation: Prithee , is this a Temple of Venus ?

sections.label: Section 1.5.285
sections.original: Fateór . ego huius fáni sacérdos clueo .
sections.translation: I will admit it ; I am styled the Priestess of this Temple . But whatever it is , it shall be done by me with a hearty welcome , so far as my means shall suffice . Come with me this way .

sections.label: Section 1.5.288b
sections.original: Amíce benígneque honórem ,
sections.translation: Kindly and attentively , mother , do you show your attentions to us .

sections.label: Section 1.5.289b
sections.original: Oportet .—
sections.translation: So I ought to do .

sections.label: Section 2.1.290
sections.original: Omníbus modis qui paúperes sunt hómines miseri vívont ,
sections.translation: Persons who are poor live wretchedly in every way , especially those who have no calling and have learned no art . Of necessity must that be deemed enough , whatever they have at home . From our garb , then , you pretty well understand how wealthy we are . These hooks and these rods here are as good to us as a calling and as our clothing .

sections.label: Section 2.1.295
sections.original: ⋯ ex urbe ad mare huc prodimus pabulatum :
sections.translation: Each day from the city do we come out hither to the sea to seek for forage . Instead of exertion in the wrestling-school and the place for exercise , we have this : sea-urchins , rock-mussels , oysters , limpets [*] , cockles , sea-nettles , sea-mussels and spotted crabs [*] , we catch . After that , we commence our fishing with the hook and among the rocks ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.300
sections.original: cibum captamus e mari : si eventus non evenit
sections.translation: and thus we take our food from out of the sea . If success does not befall us , and not any fish is taken , soaked in salt water [*] and thoroughly drenched , we quietly betake ourselves home , and without dinner go to sleep . And since the sea is now in waves so boisterous , no hopes have we ; unless we take some cockles , without a doubt we ’ ve had our dinners .

sections.label: Section 2.1.305
sections.original: nunc Venerem hanc veneremur bonam , ut nos lepide adiuerit hodie .
sections.translation: Now let ’ s adore good Venus here , that she may kindly befriend us to-day .

sections.label: Section 2.2.306
sections.original: Animum adversavi sedulo , ne erum usquam praeterirem ;
sections.translation: I ’ ve carefully given all attention that I mightn ’ t pass my master anywhere ; for when some time since he went out of the house , he said that he was going to the harbour , and he ordered me to come here to meet him at the Temple of Venus . But see , opportunely do I espy some people standing here of whom I may enquire ; I ’ ll accost them .

sections.label: Section 2.2.310
sections.original: salvete , fures maritimi , conchitae atque hamiotae ,
sections.translation: ( Goes up to the FISHERMEN .) Save you , thieves of the sea , shellfish-gatherers and hook-fishers [*] , hungry race of men , how fare ye ? How perish apace [*] ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.312
sections.original: Vt piscatorem aequomst , fame sitique speque ⋯
sections.translation: Just as befits a fisherman with hunger , thirst , and expectation .

sections.label: Section 2.2.313
sections.original: ⋯ ecquem adulescentem
sections.translation: Have you seen to-day , while you ’ ve been standing here , any young man , of courageous aspect , ruddy , stout , of genteel appearance , come by this way ,

sections.label: Section 2.2.315
sections.original: qui tres secum homines duceret chlamydatos cum machaeris ,
sections.translation: who was taking with him three men in scarfs , with swords ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.316
sections.original: Nullum istac facie ut praedicas venisse huc scimus .
sections.translation: We know of no one coming this way of that appearance which you mention .

sections.label: Section 2.2.316b
sections.original: Ecquem
sections.translation: Have you seen any old fellow , bald on the forehead and snub-nosed , of big stature , pot-bellied , with eyebrows awry , a narrow forehead , a knave , the scorn of Gods and men , a scoundrel , one full of vile dishonesty and of iniquity ,

sections.label: Section 2.2.320
sections.original: qui duceret mulierculas duas sécum satis venustas ?
sections.translation: who had along with him two very pretty-looking young women ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.321
sections.original: Cum istius modi virtutibus operisque natus qui sit ,
sections.translation: One who has been born with qualities and endowments of that sort , ’ twere really fitter for him to resort to the executioner than to the Temple of Venus .

sections.label: Section 2.2.323
sections.original: At si vidistis , dicite .
sections.translation: But tell me if you have seen him .

sections.label: Section 2.2.323b
sections.original: Huc profecto nullus venit .
sections.translation: Really , no one has passed this way . Fare you well .

sections.label: Section 2.2.324b
sections.original: Valete . credidi : factum est quod suspicabar ,
sections.translation: Fare ye well . ( Exeunt FISHERMEN .) ( to himself .) I thought so ; it has come to pass as I suspected ;

sections.label: Section 2.2.325
sections.original: data verba ero sunt , leno abit scelestus exulatum ,
sections.translation: my master has been deceived ; the cursed Procurer has taken himself off to distant lands . He has embarked on board ship , and carried the women away ; I ’ m a wizard . He invited my master here to breakfast , as well , this very spawn of wickedness . Now what is better for me than to wait here in this spot until my master comes ? At the same time , if this Priestess of Venus knows anything more ,

sections.label: Section 2.2.330
sections.original: si videro , exquisivero : faciet me certiorem .
sections.translation: if I see her , I ’ ll make enquiries ; she ’ ll give me the information .

sections.label: Section 2.3.331
sections.original: Intellego : hanc quae proxuma est víllam Veneris fano
sections.translation: I understand ; here at this cottage ( pointing to it ) , which is close by the Temple of Venus , you ’ ve requested me to knock and ask for water .

sections.label: Section 2.3.332b
sections.original: Cuia ad auris
sections.translation: Whose voice is it that has flown to my ears ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.333b
sections.original: Obsecro , quis hic loquitur ? quém ego video ?
sections.translation: Prithee , who ’ s speaking here ? Who is it that I see ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.334
sections.original: Estne Ampelisca haec quae foras e fano egreditur ?
sections.translation: Isn ’ t this Ampelisca that ’ s coming out from the Temple ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.334b
sections.original: Estne hic
sections.translation: Isn ’ t this

sections.label: Section 2.3.335
sections.original: Trachalio , quem conspicor , calator Plesidippi ?
sections.translation: Trachalio that I see , the servant of Plesidippus ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.336
sections.original: East .
sections.translation: It is she .

sections.label: Section 2.3.336b
sections.original: Is est . Trachalio , salve .
sections.translation: It is he ; Trachalio , health to you .

sections.label: Section 2.3.336c
sections.original: Salve , Ampelisca .
sections.translation: Health , Ampelisca , to you ; how fare you ?
